evals.expected_output: Shows async let approach for parallel execution, explains why the original is sequential, mentions TaskGroup as an alternative for dynamic cases, and notes structured concurrency guarantees.

assertions.name: uses-async-let
assertions.description: Response uses `async let` syntax to show parallel execution
assertions.value: async let

assertions.name: explains-sequential-problem
assertions.description: Explains why the original code runs sequentially despite using await
assertions.value: The original code runs sequentially because each await suspends and waits for the result before starting the next call

assertions.name: mentions-task-group
assertions.description: Mentions TaskGroup as an alternative for dynamic parallelism

assertions.name: shows-complete-code
assertions.description: Shows a complete, runnable code example with the parallel version
assertions.value: Provides a complete code snippet showing the parallel async let pattern with await on the collected results

assertions.name: includes-pitfalls
assertions.description: Mentions at least one common pitfall (e.g., partial failure, re-serializing, actor reentrancy)
assertions.value: Warns about a real-world gotcha such as: if one async let throws the others are cancelled, or accidentally re-serializing parallel results, or forgetting to await

evals.expected_output: Diagnoses that mixing @Observable with ObservableObject/@Published/@ObservedObject is the problem, shows the correct migration pattern, explains the Observation framework's per-property tracking advantage.

assertions.name: removes-observable-object
assertions.description: Removes ObservableObject conformance from the @Observable class
assertions.value: Shows that when using @Observable, the class should not also conform to ObservableObject

assertions.name: removes-published
assertions.description: Removes @Published property wrappers since @Observable tracks properties automatically
assertions.value: Explains that @Observable tracks property changes automatically, making @Published unnecessary and conflicting

assertions.name: uses-correct-property-wrapper
assertions.description: Uses @State or @Bindable instead of @ObservedObject for the view property

assertions.name: explains-observation-advantage
assertions.description: Explains the per-property tracking advantage of the Observation framework
assertions.value: The Observation framework tracks individual property access so only views that read a changed property re-render, unlike ObservableObject which notifies on any @Published change

assertions.name: includes-migration-checklist
assertions.description: Provides a concrete migration checklist or step-by-step migration guide
assertions.value: Lists specific migration steps like: remove ObservableObject, remove @Published, replace @StateObject with @State, replace @ObservedObject with @Bindable

assertions.name: shows-ownership-scenarios
assertions.description: Shows multiple property wrapper choices based on ownership (owned vs received vs read-only)
assertions.value: Explains that @State is for view-owned objects, @Bindable for received objects needing bindings, and plain let/var for read-only access

evals.prompt: Set up a new Swift package called `DataKit` with a library target that exports a public struct `DataFrame` with a method `filter(where:)`, and a test target that verifies filtering works. Show me the full Package.swift and file layout.
evals.expected_output: Complete Package.swift with library and test targets, source file with public DataFrame struct, test file with XCTest or Swift Testing, and directory layout.

assertions.name: has-package-swift
assertions.description: Shows a valid Package.swift manifest
assertions.value: Package(

assertions.name: has-library-target
assertions.description: Defines a library target in Package.swift
assertions.value: .library(

assertions.name: has-test-target
assertions.description: Defines a test target in Package.swift
assertions.value: .testTarget(

assertions.name: public-struct
assertions.description: DataFrame struct is marked public
assertions.value: public struct DataFrame

assertions.name: filter-method
assertions.description: DataFrame has a filter(where:) method
assertions.value: Shows a public filter method on DataFrame that takes a where parameter (a closure predicate) to filter rows

assertions.name: shows-directory-layout
assertions.description: Shows the expected directory structure
assertions.value: Shows Sources/DataKit/ and Tests/DataKitTests/ directory structure

assertions.name: test-imports-module
assertions.description: Test file imports DataKit

assertions.name: includes-design-rationale
assertions.description: Explains design decisions (why struct, why public, target boundaries, etc.)
assertions.value: Includes some design rationale explaining choices like why DataFrame is a struct (value semantics), why filter returns a new instance, or why the targets are structured this way

evals.expected_output: Systematic migration approach: make the class an actor or add proper isolation, explain why @unchecked Sendable is dangerous, show multiple fix strategies with tradeoffs, include migration checklist.

assertions.name: shows-actor-solution
assertions.description: Shows converting the class to an actor as a primary solution
assertions.value: actor

assertions.name: warns-unchecked-sendable
assertions.description: Explicitly warns against @unchecked Sendable as a lazy fix
assertions.value: Warns that @unchecked Sendable silences the compiler but doesn't make the type safe, and should be a last resort

assertions.name: shows-multiple-strategies
assertions.description: Presents multiple fix strategies (actor, @MainActor, Sendable conformance, value type) with guidance on when to use each
assertions.value: Shows at least 3 different approaches to fixing Sendable violations and explains when each is appropriate

assertions.name: includes-tradeoff-table
assertions.description: Includes a comparison/tradeoff table for the different approaches
assertions.value: Contains a structured table or matrix comparing the fix strategies (e.g., actor vs struct vs @MainActor) on dimensions like thread safety, performance, API impact

assertions.name: systematic-migration-steps
assertions.description: Provides systematic migration steps rather than just fixing the one example
assertions.value: Provides a general migration checklist or systematic approach that applies to an entire codebase, not just the single NetworkManager example

assertions.name: version-awareness
assertions.description: Mentions Swift version considerations (strict concurrency levels, Swift 5 vs 6)
assertions.value: Mentions Swift 5 vs 6 strict concurrency checking levels or the progression from warnings to errors

evals.expected_output: Comparison of some vs any with performance implications, concrete before/after refactoring, guidance on when any is correct, generics as an alternative.

assertions.name: explains-some-vs-any
assertions.description: Explains the difference between some and any with performance implications
assertions.value: Explains that 'some' is a compile-time opaque type with no existential overhead while 'any' is a runtime box with indirection and allocation costs

assertions.name: shows-generic-alternative
assertions.description: Shows generics as an alternative to both some and any
assertions.value: Shows a generic function with a Drawable constraint (e.g., func process<T: Drawable> or similar generic syntax) as an alternative to existentials

assertions.name: shows-when-any-is-correct
assertions.description: Explicitly identifies when any is the RIGHT choice (heterogeneous collections, type erasure)
assertions.value: Identifies specific scenarios where any is correct, such as heterogeneous arrays where different concrete types are stored together

assertions.name: includes-decision-guide
assertions.description: Includes a decision table/flowchart for choosing between some, any, and generics
assertions.value: Contains a structured decision guide (table, flowchart, or if-then list) for when to use some vs any vs generics

assertions.name: concrete-refactoring
assertions.description: Shows concrete before/after code for at least 2 of the 3 functions
assertions.value: Provides refactored versions of at least 2 of the 3 example functions (render, firstShape, process) with concrete code

evals.expected_output: Diagnoses actor reentrancy as the root cause, explains that state can change across await, shows fix pattern with in-flight tracking, warns about related pitfalls.

assertions.name: identifies-reentrancy
assertions.description: Identifies actor reentrancy as the root cause by name

assertions.name: explains-suspension-state-change
assertions.description: Explains that state can change across an await point inside an actor
assertions.value: Explains that when the actor suspends at the await point, another call can enter the actor and the store may not have the key yet, so multiple fetches for the same key can occur

assertions.name: shows-inflight-tracking
assertions.description: Shows a fix using in-flight request tracking (continuations, tasks, or similar)
assertions.value: Shows a fix pattern where in-progress requests are tracked (e.g., a dictionary of Task or continuation) so subsequent callers for the same key wait on the existing request instead of starting a new one

assertions.name: warns-related-pitfalls
assertions.description: Warns about related pitfalls beyond just the immediate fix
assertions.value: Mentions at least one related pitfall such as: Task cancellation handling, memory leaks from stored Tasks, stale cache invalidation, or deadlock risks

assertions.name: shows-complete-fixed-code
assertions.description: Shows a complete working version of the fixed actor
assertions.value: Provides a complete, runnable implementation of the fixed DataCache actor with in-flight tracking
